serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 250 ml can of energy drink, the most common single-serve commercial format available in Singapore and globally.

nutrient_source.title: Energy drink — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for generic energy drink (cr_id D05020005). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glucose drinks and sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages — GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to generic sugar-sweetened energy drink
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a generic energy drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the closest analogous approved-source entry: sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink (GI ~63–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The dominant glycaemic driver is sucrose/glucose at 10.39 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre, consistent with a medium-to-high GI profile. Serving GL calculated as: GI 68 × (carbohydrate_g per serving 34.0 g) / 100 = 23.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.39 g/100 g) in a beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g sugar threshold for beverages, and caffeine content (typically 30–40 mg/100 ml) causes acute blood pressure elevation independent of sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid energy drinks entirely; the combination of sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention and caffeine-induced sympathetic activation independently elevates blood pressure risk beyond what the low sodium content (26 mg/100 g) would suggest.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.39 g sugar/100 g in liquid form with zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; it qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and the absence of fibre means glucose absorption is unattenuated, producing rapid and pronounced blood glucose spikes that undermine glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened energy drinks is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased cardiovascular event risk, and caffeine-related arrhythmia risk; the high sugar load (10.39 g/100 g) with zero fibre drives hepatic de novo lipogenesis.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid energy drinks; the combination of high sugar, caffeine, and taurine in a liquid ultra-processed format is associated with adverse lipid profiles, elevated blood pressure, and increased risk of cardiac arrhythmia with regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (10.39 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes systemic inflammatory mediators relevant to RA; no omega-3, polyphenols, or other anti-inflammatory components are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid energy drinks; the pro-inflammatory sugar load and absence of any anti-inflammatory nutritional component make this beverage counterproductive to RA management, and high caffeine may interact with RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Energy drink is a commercially manufactured, carbonated, ultra-processed beverage formulated with sugar, caffeine, taurine, and B-vitamins, designed to provide a short-term boost in alertness and physical energy. Per 100 g, it delivers 64 kcal, 13.6 g carbohydrate (of which 10.39 g is sugar), 0.78 g protein, 0.73 g fat, and 26 mg sodium, with zero dietary fibre. The beverage is sweet and lightly acidic in taste, with a characteristic carbonated mouthfeel. It is sold globally in standard 250 ml cans and is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Clinically, energy drinks are classified as sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) due to their sugar content exceeding 5 g/100 g in liquid form; this classification triggers hard dietary gates for diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management. The liquid sugar matrix with zero fibre produces rapid postprandial glucose excursions, making this beverage unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Caffeine content (typically 30–40 mg/100 ml) independently elevates blood pressure and poses arrhythmia risk for cardiovascular patients. The glycaemic index is estimated at 68 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycaemic load of approximately 23.1 per standard 250 ml serving — a clinically significant GL for a single beverage. No anti-inflammatory nutrients are present, making this beverage unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis management. The dietary fibre content is confirmed zero by the HPB Singapore source.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in an energy drink?
Is energy drink bad for blood pressure?
Can I drink energy drinks if I have hypertension?
Is energy drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05020005) with confidence 0.95. The HPB record is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved-source equivalent (sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage), which is the standard defensible approach when no direct GI entry exists for a generic commercial product.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0 g/100 g) per HPB Singapore source — this is consistent with the formulation of commercial energy drinks which contain no plant-based fibre components. Calcium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g and iron as 0.0 mg/100 g per HPB source; these are confirmed zero values for this product category. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this generic energy drink entry are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record as of 2026-04-16. While energy drinks typically contain B-vitamins by formulation, specific per-100 g values are not available in the HPB source for this generic record and cannot be reliably imputed without a product-specific label. GI estimated at 68 (medium) using analogous sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for a generic energy drink. GL per 250 ml serving calculated as: 68 × (13.6 g/100 g × 250 g / 100) = 68 × 34.0 / 100 = 23.1. Halal status not confirmed for generic category record; individual branded products may carry halal certification. The low_calorie dietary tag from the input has been reviewed: at 64 kcal/100 g (160 kcal per 250 ml can), this product is not low-calorie by standard definitions and that tag has been removed from dietary_flags.
